blocks.text: Chapter 4.
Define the Real Problem, Then Widen Your Options
The ideas in 30 seconds
A decision requires conscious evaluation of options; most of what we call decisions are unexamined choices driven by the defaults, and only a deliberate process earns the name.

The person making the decision must own the problem definition — inherited framings from bosses, colleagues, or first speakers usually describe symptoms, politics, or someone else's convenience, not the real problem.
Solving symptoms feels productive but guarantees the problem returns; the root cause is found by asking what would have to be true for the problem not to exist at all.
Writing the problem down and holding separate meetings for problem-definition and problem-solving are cheap safeguards that stop teams from racing to solve the wrong thing.
Good options come from imagining bad outcomes, asking "and then what?" past first-order consequences, and refusing to decide until you have at least three real alternatives.
Most either/or dilemmas are failures of imagination — the strongest decision-makers convert binary trade-offs into both-and solutions through sequencing, experimentation, or reframing.
A Decision Is Not the Same Thing as a Choice

Parrish draws a sharp line between two things we casually lump together. A choice is simply picking an option — something you can do reflexively, on autopilot, with the emotion, ego, social, and inertia defaults (covered in chapter 1) doing the picking for you. A decision is a choice that involves conscious thought: you evaluated options, weighed them against reality, and selected deliberately. By this definition, most people make very few actual decisions.

The distinction matters because it defines what "deciding well" even means. If you reacted without thinking, you didn't decide badly — you didn't decide at all. Judgment lives entirely in the deliberate process, and that process has distinct stages: define the problem, explore possible solutions, evaluate the options, and then act. This chapter covers the first two stages; the following chapters cover evaluation, execution, and learning.

The order of the stages is not decorative. Each stage's quality caps the quality of everything downstream: brilliant evaluation of options generated for the wrong problem still produces the wrong answer. Yet in practice, people invert the effort — they rush through problem definition in seconds and spend their energy debating solutions. Parrish's observation from working with executives is that the best decision-makers do the opposite. They appear slow at the front of the process because they refuse to solve anything until they're sure what they're solving, and then move fast through everything else.

The defaults are the enemy at every stage. The social default pushes you to accept the room's framing; ego pushes you to look decisive rather than be right; inertia pushes you toward the options already on the table; emotion pushes you to relieve discomfort now. The principles and safeguards below exist to hold the process open long enough for thinking to happen.
Own the Framing: The Definition Principle

The definition principle states that the decision-maker is responsible for defining the problem — personally, and never by simply adopting the first framing offered. This sounds obvious and is violated constantly. In most organizations, whoever speaks first frames the problem, and everyone else's cognition snaps to that frame. From that moment the meeting is about solutions to a definition nobody vetted.

Inherited framings are unreliable for predictable reasons. The person handing you the problem often describes it in terms of the solution they already prefer ("we need to hire more salespeople" is a solution wearing a problem costume). Or they describe the part of the problem that touches their department. Or they repeat how the problem was framed last time, whether or not it was solved. Accepting these framings is the social default at work: it feels cooperative and efficient, and it quietly hands your judgment to someone who may have thought about the issue less than you're about to.

Owning the definition means doing your own work: talking to the people closest to the problem rather than the people closest to you, asking what's actually happening versus what's being reported, and testing whether the stated problem is the real one. Parrish's test for a good problem statement is that it identifies what you actually want to achieve, not merely what you want to stop feeling. "Sales are down" is an observation; "our onboarding loses 40 percent of trial users before they experience the core feature" is a problem you can attack.

There's a status trap here too. Ego wants to skip to solutions because proposing solutions looks smart, while asking basic questions about the problem feels naive. Parrish's counter is that in reality the highest-leverage contribution in most rooms is the person willing to ask whether the group is solving the right problem — a question that costs minutes and routinely saves months.
Attack the Root, Not the Symptom

The root cause principle: identify the root cause of the problem, not merely the symptom it presents. Symptom-solving is seductive because it produces visible, immediate relief — and it guarantees you'll be solving the same problem again soon, usually after it has compounded.

Parrish's medical analogy makes the distinction concrete: treating symptoms is taking painkillers for a condition that needs surgery. You feel better, the underlying disease progresses, and each round of relief makes the eventual reckoning worse. Organizations institutionalize this pattern because they reward firefighting. The person who heroically handles the crisis gets praised and promoted; the person who quietly ensures the crisis never happens produces no visible drama and gets overlooked. So everyone learns to let fires start.

The diagnostic question Parrish offers for finding the root is: "What would have to be true for this problem not to exist in the first place?" This question forces you underneath the presenting complaint. If the problem is "we keep missing deadlines," asking what would have to be true for deadlines never to be missed surfaces candidates — estimates are fantasy, scope changes mid-project, one team is a bottleneck — each of which is a different, more fundamental problem than the one you started with.

Keep asking until the answers stop changing. The first answer is usually another symptom; the real cause tends to sit two or three layers down, and it's often less flattering than the surface story — which is exactly why the ego default steers people away from it. A team that frames its problem as "the market turned" never has to examine whether its product stopped being good.

The payoff for this discipline is asymmetric. Solving a symptom buys you weeks; solving a root cause deletes an entire recurring category of problems, along with all the future time and attention they would have consumed.
Two Safeguards: Write the Problem Down, and Hold Two Meetings
Knowing the principles isn't enough, because in the moment the defaults will still rush you toward solutions. Parrish prescribes two structural safeguards.

First, write the problem down. A problem statement that exists only in your head can stay comfortably vague; writing forces precision. The act exposes whether you actually understand the problem or have only been circling it — if you can't state it in a sentence or two, you don't yet understand it. A written statement also becomes a shareable object: colleagues can challenge it, sharpen it, and point out that it describes a symptom. And it persists, so that days later you can check whether the solution being built still addresses the problem as defined, or whether the work has silently drifted toward solving something easier.

Second, separate the problem-definition meeting from the problem-solving meeting. Hold one meeting whose only permitted output is an agreed statement of the problem — no solutions allowed — and a second meeting, ideally at least a day later, to generate and debate solutions. This feels inefficient and is the opposite. The separation blocks the room's strongest voice from framing and solving in one breath, gives the social default nothing to converge on prematurely, and lets people's subconscious work on the problem overnight. Parrish notes that sleeping on a well-defined problem reliably produces better options than brainstorming in the same hour the problem was first named.

Both safeguards trade a trivial amount of time for protection against the most expensive failure in decision-making: an elegant, well-executed solution to the wrong problem. The short-term thinker sees the extra meeting as waste; the clear thinker sees that time spent defining the problem is recovered many times over in the solving.
Generate Options Like a Pessimist: Bad Outcomes and Second-Level Thinking
Once the problem is defined, the quality of your decision is capped by the quality of the options you generate. Two thinking tools raise that ceiling.

The bad outcome principle: don't only imagine the futures you want — deliberately imagine the ways each option could go wrong, and work out what you'd do if it did. Most people generate options while picturing success, which means their option set is optimized for a future that may not arrive. Asking "what does this look like if it fails?" does two things: it filters out options whose failure modes you can't survive, and it lets you pre-build responses to trouble while you're calm, rather than improvising them mid-crisis with your emotion default in charge. (Chapter 6 extends this into formal premortems and tripwires at the execution stage.)

The second tool is second-level thinking — relentlessly asking "and then what?" First-level thinking evaluates an option by its immediate consequence; second-level thinking follows the chain. Parrish's example is deliberately trivial: eat the chocolate bar. First level: it tastes good now. And then what? A sugar crash, another small vote for a habit, and — repeated daily — a different body and health trajectory a year out. The same structure governs consequential decisions: the option that looks best at level one (cut the price, ship the shortcut, avoid the hard conversation) is frequently the one that looks worst at level two or three, because immediate consequences and eventual consequences often point in opposite directions.

Neither tool requires intelligence so much as discipline. The defaults all bias you toward first-level, best-case evaluation because it feels good and resolves uncertainty quickly. Asking "and then what?" and "what if this goes badly?" is uncomfortable precisely because it keeps the decision open — which is the point.
Never Accept a Binary: Three-Plus Options and Both-And

When someone presents you with two options, the real message is usually that they stopped thinking early. Parrish's safeguard is blunt: force yourself to generate at least three options before deciding. Binary framings ("either we cut costs or we miss the quarter") feel decisive but are almost always artifacts of limited imagination, time pressure, or someone's agenda. The requirement of a third option breaks the spell — it forces genuine exploration rather than a coin flip dressed up as analysis.

A companion technique for when you feel stuck with the options in front of you: imagine that none of them is available. Ask, "What would I do if I couldn't choose any of my current options?" Removing the existing options from consideration forces the search to restart, and the option generated under that constraint is frequently better than everything on the original list — proof of how quickly inertia anchors us to whatever appeared first.

The deepest move in this section is dissolving the dilemma itself. Both-and thinking treats "either/or" as a challenge rather than a constraint: instead of choosing between X and Y, ask what it would take to get both. Often the answer is sequencing (do a cheap, fast version of one option first to buy information, then pursue the other), or a small experiment that tests both paths before committing fully, or a reframe that reveals the two options were never actually exclusive — they only appeared so within the inherited framing. Parrish's observation is that exceptional decision-makers are distinguished less by how they choose between hard trade-offs than by how often they find "a secret third option that combines the best of both."
Widening options costs little and compounds. You can only ever choose from the set you generated — a mediocre choice from five strong options beats a perfect choice between two weak ones.
In practice
Before your next significant decision, write a one-to-two-sentence problem statement and show it to someone close to the problem; revise until they agree it names the real issue, not a symptom.
When handed a problem, ask "What would have to be true for this problem not to exist?" at least twice before discussing any solution.
For your next team decision, run two meetings: one that only defines the problem (ban solution talk), and a solutions meeting at least one day later.
For each option you're considering, write one sentence on how it fails and answer "and then what?" for at least two levels of consequences.
Refuse to decide between two options — generate a third, and if you're stuck, ask what you'd do if every current option were off the table.
Take one either/or dilemma you're facing this week and spend fifteen minutes designing a both-and: sequence the options, run a cheap test of one, or reframe until the conflict dissolves.
